focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Above Patent Row, the room Gideon kept at Verdancy House sat high enough to miss the gutter stink for three breaths and no more. After that the city got in anyway: yeast, wet paper, lamp mash warming in cellar pipes, the sweet edge of rot that all rich men in Nacre Bend pretended was someone else's climate. Gideon had arranged his office as a rebuttal. The curtains were cream instead of greened white. The desk was walnut instead of pine. Even the ink smelled dearer, iron and clove instead of the ordinary sour black used by printers and creditors.

When Silas came in the next day, the clerk who admitted him wore a mourning brooch shaped like a tiny anchor and kept cracking fennel seeds between her back teeth as if the office itself gave her heartburn. She set a folded paper beside Gideon's hand and said, "Mr. Vale," with a look that suggested she had been told what kind of man he was and had improved the picture privately.

Gideon waited until the door shut before he touched the paper. He was gloved as usual, pearl gray kid, clean enough to insult half the city on sight.

"You look tired," he said.

"I got laughed at in three wards on my way here. It takes a certain energy."

"Public amusement is not fatal. It is often preparatory."

Silas dropped into the chair opposite him without being asked. The leather gave a little squeak under his damp coat. "If that's your sermon on the launch, save it. I was there for the collection."

Gideon slid the folded paper across the desk. It was the Havelock Pigments receipt Gideon had kept from the first week's advance: forty-three crowns, settled in full. The owner's greasy thumbprint sat at the bottom like a bruise.

The old relief twitched through him anyway, clean and ugly. Havelock had stopped sending boys past his sh...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ckery had the best legs in Nacre Bend.

Silas looked up at the wall.

Celadon Crown was still there, huge and undeniable. He had painted it well. His own good lettering was the insult. The capitals had weight, curve, proportion, a professional confidence no crowd could deny him. But the city had written through him. Pink burned in the counters. Rope green thickened at the bases. Blue held the upper brick like evening in a mourning room. White flashed where the heat had gone wrong. The letters looked less like a decree than like something caught in crossfire.

Gideon followed his gaze.

"This can still be placed," he said.

Silas turned. "Placed where?"

"Everywhere repetition is better managed."

The answer chilled him more than rage would have. Gideon was not surrendering. He was taking notes.

The lone wet fleck on Gideon's cuff had turned, during all this, from pale green to a sharp accusing pink.

Silas almost told him. Instead he watched Gideon notice it, and watched the man's face harden by one careful degree.

Down the slope, Damaris's runner vanished into press alley. The crowd was already carrying the argument outward in clumps, each cluster with its own preferred word. Handbills stuck to boots. Paper crowns drifted in the gutter. Celadon wash ran through the channels toward Ferrymouth and the lower wards in bright infected threads.

Silas stood in the middle of the emptied ceremony with paint drying on his knuckles and other people's names drying on his work. He could already see the break traveling: into Damaris's paper by breakfast, into the lower wards sooner than that, into Ferrymouth kitchens and lamp trays before dark. Into Junie's, if the gutter held its line.

He should have gone downhill before the gutter did.

Patent Row had seen him lose the street.
